---
title: "6.1 Exchange Economies"
---

# 6.1 Exchange Economies

> **MWG Reference:** Ch. 15, §15.A–15.C, pp. 505–545

## Context

The pure exchange economy is the simplest general equilibrium model: there is no production, only redistribution of a fixed aggregate endowment through trade. Despite its simplicity, the exchange economy contains all the essential conceptual apparatus: competitive equilibrium, excess demand, Walras' Law, price normalization, and the geometric intuition of the Edgeworth box. These are the foundations on which the Arrow-Debreu production economy and the welfare theorems are built.

## Definitions

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition 6.1.1 — Pure Exchange Economy
A **pure exchange economy** $\mathcal{E}$ consists of:
- $I = \{1, \ldots, n\}$ consumers and $L$ goods
- Consumer $i$'s **endowment** $\omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^L_+$: the bundle they bring to the market
- Consumer $i$'s preference relation $\succeq_i$ (or utility function $u_i: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}$) over consumption bundles
- **Total (aggregate) endowment:** $\bar{\omega} = \sum_{i=1}^n \omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}$
:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition 6.1.2 — Walrasian (Competitive) Equilibrium
A **Walrasian equilibrium** of $\mathcal{E}$ is a pair $(x^*, p^*)$ where $x^* = (x_1^*, \ldots, x_n^*)$ and $p^* \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}$ such that:
1. **Individual optimality:** For each $i$, $x_i^* \in x_i(p^*, p^* \cdot \omega_i)$, where $x_i(p, w)$ is consumer $i$'s Walrasian demand correspondence at price $p$ and wealth $w$
2. **Market clearing:** $\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^* = \bar{\omega}$ (the allocation is feasible and exhausts the endowment)
:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition 6.1.3 — Excess Demand
The **excess demand function** (or correspondence) is
$$z(p) = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) - \bar{\omega}.$$
Equilibrium requires $z(p^*) = 0_{\mathbb{R}^L}$ (every market clears).
:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition 6.1.4 — Edgeworth Box
In a two-consumer, two-good economy with total endowment $\bar{\omega} = (\bar{\omega}_1, \bar{\omega}_2)$, the **Edgeworth box** is the rectangle of feasible allocations:
$$\mathcal{F} = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^4_+ : x_1 + x_2 = \bar{\omega}\}.$$
Consumer 1's origin is the southwest corner; consumer 2's origin is the northeast corner. Any point $(x_1, x_2) \in \mathcal{F}$ represents a feasible allocation.
:::


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Definition 6.1.5 — Pareto Set and Contract Curve
An allocation $(x_1, x_2) \in \mathcal{F}$ is **Pareto optimal** if there is no other feasible $(x_1', x_2')$ such that $u_i(x_i') \geq u_i(x_i)$ for all $i$ and $u_j(x_j') > u_j(x_j)$ for some $j$.

The **contract curve** (or Pareto set) is the set of all Pareto optimal allocations in the Edgeworth box. In the interior, it consists of points where the consumers' indifference curves are tangent, i.e., $MRS_1 = MRS_2$.
:::


## Main Results

::: {.callout-important icon=false}
## Theorem 6.1.1 — Walras' Law
If each consumer's preference relation $\succeq_i$ satisfies **local nonsatiation**, then the excess demand function satisfies:
$$p \cdot z(p) = 0 \quad \text{for all } p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}.$$
:::


::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse="true"}
## Proof
By local nonsatiation, each consumer exhausts their budget at any optimal choice:
$$p \cdot x_i^*(p, p \cdot \omega_i) = p \cdot \omega_i \quad \forall i.$$
(If the budget constraint were not binding, there would exist a preferred bundle arbitrarily close to $x_i^*$, contradicting optimality.)

Summing over all consumers:
$$\sum_{i=1}^n p \cdot x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) = \sum_{i=1}^n p \cdot \omega_i.$$
Rearranging:
$$p \cdot \left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) - \sum_{i=1}^n \omega_i\right) = 0.$$
By Definition 6.1.3, this is $p \cdot z(p) = 0$. $\blacksquare$
:::


::: {.callout-caution icon=false}
## Remark
Walras' Law has a crucial implication for market clearing. If there are $L$ goods and $L-1$ markets clear (i.e., $z_\ell(p^*) = 0$ for $\ell = 1, \ldots, L-1$), then:
$$p^* \cdot z(p^*) = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{\ell=1}^L p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) = 0 \Rightarrow p_L^* z_L(p^*) = 0.$$
Since $p_L^* > 0$ in equilibrium, it follows that $z_L(p^*) = 0$. The $L$-th market clears automatically. This reduces the number of independent equilibrium conditions to $L-1$.
:::


::: {.callout-important icon=false}
## Theorem 6.1.2 — Properties of Excess Demand
Under standard assumptions (continuity and local nonsatiation of preferences, positive endowments), the excess demand function $z: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \to \mathbb{R}^L$ satisfies:
1. **Walras' Law:** $p \cdot z(p) = 0$ for all $p \gg 0$
2. **Homogeneity of degree zero:** $z(\lambda p) = z(p)$ for all $\lambda > 0$
3. **Continuity:** $z$ is continuous on $\mathbb{R}^L_{++}$
4. **Boundary condition:** As $p_\ell \to 0$, $z_\ell(p) \to +\infty$ (demand for a free good is unbounded)
:::


::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse="true"}
## Proof
**Homogeneity:** Each individual demand $x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i)$ is homogeneous of degree zero in $(p, w)$ since $w_i = p \cdot \omega_i$ scales proportionally with $p$. Therefore $x_i(\lambda p, \lambda p \cdot \omega_i) = x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i)$, and so $z(\lambda p) = z(p)$.

**Continuity:** Under standard preference assumptions (continuous, strictly convex), Walrasian demand $x_i(p, w)$ is continuous. Continuity of $z$ follows from continuity of the sum.

**Boundary condition:** As $p_\ell \to 0$, good $\ell$ becomes free. By local nonsatiation, each consumer wants more of every good (or at worst, is indifferent). With free good $\ell$, the budget set becomes effectively unconstrained in dimension $\ell$, and demand for $\ell$ grows without bound. Formally, the Walrasian demand $x_{i\ell}(p, w) \to +\infty$ as $p_\ell \to 0^+$ for any fixed $w$ (since the wealth buys infinite units of good $\ell$). Therefore $z_\ell(p) \to +\infty$. $\blacksquare$
:::


::: {.callout-important icon=false}
## Theorem 6.1.3 — Price Normalization
Due to homogeneity of degree zero, it is without loss of generality to restrict attention to prices in the unit simplex:
$$\Delta^{L-1} = \left\{p \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : \sum_{\ell=1}^L p_\ell = 1\right\}.$$
Any equilibrium price vector $p^* \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}$ corresponds to a normalized price $p^*/\|p^*\|_1 \in \Delta^{L-1}$ that is also an equilibrium (since excess demand is unchanged).
:::


## The Edgeworth Box: Geometric Intuition

::: {.callout-note icon=false appearance="simple"}
## Example 6.1.1 — Walrasian Equilibrium in the Edgeworth Box
Consider 2 consumers, 2 goods: $u_1(x_{11}, x_{12}) = x_{11}^\alpha x_{12}^{1-\alpha}$, $u_2(x_{21}, x_{22}) = x_{21}^\beta x_{22}^{1-\beta}$. Endowments: $\omega_1 = (1, 0)$, $\omega_2 = (0, 1)$.

**Walrasian demand:** With Cobb-Douglas preferences, consumer $i$'s demand for good $\ell$ is a fixed fraction of wealth:
$$x_{11}(p, w_1) = \frac{\alpha w_1}{p_1} = \frac{\alpha p_1}{p_1} = \alpha, \quad x_{12}(p, w_1) = \frac{(1-\alpha)p_1}{p_2}.$$
$$x_{21}(p, w_2) = \frac{\beta p_2}{p_1}, \quad x_{22}(p, w_2) = \frac{(1-\beta)p_2}{p_2} = 1-\beta.$$

**Market clearing for good 1:** $x_{11} + x_{21} = 1$:
$$\alpha + \frac{\beta p_2}{p_1} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{1-\alpha}{\beta}.$$

**Equilibrium price ratio:** $p_1/p_2 = \beta/(1-\alpha)$.

**Equilibrium allocation:** $x_1^* = (\alpha, (1-\alpha)\beta/\beta) = (\alpha, 1-\alpha)$ and $x_2^* = (1-\alpha, \beta)$... adjusting: $x_{12}^* = (1-\alpha)p_1/p_2 = (1-\alpha) \cdot \beta/(1-\alpha) = \beta$, so $x_1^* = (\alpha, \beta)$ and... Let us recompute. With $p_2/p_1 = (1-\alpha)/\beta$:
- $w_1 = p_1 \cdot 1 = p_1$; $x_{11}^* = \alpha$, $x_{12}^* = (1-\alpha)p_1/p_2 = (1-\alpha)\beta/(1-\alpha) = \beta$.
- $w_2 = p_2 \cdot 1 = p_2$; $x_{21}^* = \beta p_2/p_1 = \beta(1-\alpha)/\beta = 1-\alpha$, $x_{22}^* = 1-\beta$.

Equilibrium allocation: $x_1^* = (\alpha, \beta)$, $x_2^* = (1-\alpha, 1-\beta)$. Market clearing: $\alpha + (1-\alpha) = 1$ and $\beta + (1-\beta) = 1$. Confirmed.
:::


## Core and Walrasian Equilibrium

The **core** of the exchange economy is the set of feasible allocations that cannot be blocked by any coalition: allocation $x$ is in the core if no subset $S \subseteq I$ can redistribute their endowments $\sum_{i \in S}\omega_i$ to make all members strictly better off.

Every Walrasian equilibrium allocation is in the core (this follows from the First Welfare Theorem plus the ability of blocking coalitions to use only their own endowments). The **core convergence theorem** (Edgeworth, formalized by Debreu-Scarf 1963) states that as the economy is replicated infinitely many times, the core shrinks to the set of Walrasian allocations.

## Cross-References

- [6.2 Arrow Debreu Economy](6.2_Arrow_Debreu_Economy.qmd) — Extends to production; same excess demand framework
- [6.3 Existence of Equilibrium](6.3_Existence_of_Equilibrium.qmd) — Existence proof uses excess demand properties
- [6.4 Welfare Theorems](6.4_Welfare_Theorems.qmd) — First WT uses Walrasian equilibrium definition; Second WT shows converse
- [Index](../Part_II_Consumer_Theory/Index.qmd) — Walrasian demand $x_i(p, w)$ is the consumer theory object
